title: Mobile Shop Router Table
summary: A rolling 2x4 router table with a doubled plywood top, t-track slots for fences and featherboards, and a built-in safety switch.
description:
A handheld router works fine free-hand, but mounting it upside-down in a table turns it into a much more capable stationary tool — this base is what makes that swap practical in a small shop, since it rolls out of the way on casters when you're done.

Each leg is actually **two 2x4s, dadoed and paired into a solid 3"x3" post**, rather than a single 2x4 — a plain 2x4 leg would be noticeably less rigid under the side-load a router table sees during a heavy pass, and the doubled leg costs almost nothing extra in material.

The top is two layers of 3/4" plywood glued together with a laminate surface, which gives a dead-flat, low-friction table for feeding stock across — a single layer of plywood alone tends to telegraph any waviness in the sheet straight into your cuts.

Don't skip the **safety switch.** A router buried inside a cabinet with its own on/off switch out of reach is a bad combination the first time something needs to stop immediately.

tools.note: Dadoes the leg pairs and cuts the t-track grooves.

cutList.note: Dadoed 3/4in deep x 3in wide and paired so two boards form one solid 3x3in leg.
Glued together into one doubled top with a laminate surface.

steps.title: Build the base
steps.body: Dado each leg pair 3/4in deep and 3in wide so two 2x4s combine into a solid 3x3in leg. Build the 24in-deep, 36in-high base from the legs and rails, gluing and screwing it together.

steps.title: Add the switch and casters
steps.body: Attach the safety switch to the base and mount the four casters underneath.

steps.title: Build the top
steps.body: Glue two layers of 3/4" plywood together with a laminate surface on top, and cut dados for the t-track sections.

steps.title: Cut the insert opening
steps.body: Rout the opening for the router insert plate and install the plate.

steps.title: Install the t-track and fence
steps.body: Install the t-track sections into the top, then build an adjustable fence from plywood, locking it down with knobs, t-track bolts, and carriage bolts.
